scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. Yesterday in ED majors I assessed a 62-year-old man with pleuritic chest pain. I considered ACS, PE and pneumonia, used Wells score, reviewed ECG and CXR, discussed with my consultant, and arranged CTPA. I learned to state my risk assessment more clearly when escalating.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a 62-year-old with pleuritic chest pain, formulated a differential diagnosis, used a clinical decision tool (Wells score), reviewed imaging, and discussed the case retrospectively with a consultant.
The trainee reflects on a key non-technical learning point from this case: the need to state their risk assessment more clearly when escalating care to senior colleagues.

draft_fields.clinical_reasoning:
I assessed a 62-year-old man presenting with pleuritic chest pain. My differential diagnoses included acute coronary syndrome (ACS), pulmonary embolism (PE), and pneumonia.

To risk-stratify the patient, I calculated a Wells score, reviewed the electrocardiogram (ECG), and examined the chest X-ray (CXR). Based on my assessment, I discussed the case with the ED consultant and arranged a CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) to investigate for a PE.
draft_fields.reflection: This case of structured escalation. I learned that I need to state my risk assessment and pre-test probability more clearly when discussing and escalating cases with senior colleagues.
key_capabilities: SLO1 KC1: to be expert in assessing and managing all adult patients attending the ED. These capabilities will apply to patients attending with both physical and psychological ill health (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. Today I performed DC cardioversion for unstable atrial fibrillation as a formal DOPS. My consultant observed each step directly. I checked consent, monitoring and sedation plan, delivered a synchronised shock, and reflected on calling for airway support earlier.

recommended_forms.rationale: Directly observed hands-on procedure with senior supervision; DOPS is the specific assessed-procedure form.
The performed procedure can also be recorded in the procedural log.
Feedback/reflection was included alongside the procedural assessment.

draft_fields.trainee_performance: I performed a synchronised DC cardioversion under the direct observation of my consultant for a formal DOPS. I checked the patient's consent, ensured appropriate monitoring was in place, and established a clear sedation plan before delivering the synchronised shock.
draft_fields.reflection: The procedure was successful, but on reflection, I should have called for airway support earlier in the process to maximum safety during sedation. In future cases, I will airway support is present or immediately available before starting the sedation plan.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC2: be expert in fluid management and circulatory support in critically ill patients (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC3: manage all the life-threatening conditions including peri-arrest & arrest situations in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.form_name: Direct Observation of Procedural Skills - ACCS
scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. During my ACCS anaesthetic placement I inserted a chest drain using Seldinger technique as an observed DOPS on 5 July 2026. My supervisor watched the procedure. I confirmed landmarks, used sterile technique and reflected on slowing down before dilating.

recommended_forms.rationale: ACCS procedural skill with direct observation; use the ACCS DOPS form.
The same ACCS procedure can also be recorded in the ACCS procedural log.

draft_fields.case_observed: During my ACCS anaesthetic placement, I inserted a chest drain using the Seldinger technique under the direct observation of my supervisor. I confirmed the anatomical landmarks, maintained a strict sterile technique throughout, and successfully completed the procedure.
draft_fields.reflection: I need to I deliberately slow down my technique before dilating. Taking a conscious pause at this step helps confirm correct positioning and the tract is adequately prepared, reducing the risk of displacement or tissue trauma.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. A consultant directly observed me taking a focused history and examination from a 7-year-old with wheeze in paediatric ED. I explained the plan to the parent and received feedback on structuring my assessment.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee was directly observed by a consultant while taking a history, performing an examination, and explaining the plan to a parent, making Mini-CEX the most appropriate form.

draft_fields.clinical_reasoning: I took a focused history and performed a clinical examination on a 7-year-old child presenting to the paediatric ED with a wheeze.
draft_fields.reflection: The consultant directly observed the encounter and provided feedback on structuring my assessment. I will use this feedback to refine my approach to paediatric history taking and examination in future consultations.
key_capabilities: SLO5 KC1: be expert in assessing and managing all children and young adult patients attending the ED (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. On an evening acute take I managed six patients across majors and resus, including sepsis, chest pain and a frail fall. My consultant observed my prioritisation, handover and escalation decisions over the shift.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a cohort of six patients across majors and resus during an acute take, with a consultant observing their prioritisation, management, and escalation decisions over the shift.
A consultant observed the trainee's work and non-technical skills (prioritisation, handover, escalation, and management) across a substantial portion of a clinical shift.

draft_fields.cases_observed: During an evening acute take, I managed six patients across majors and resus. The cases included a patient presenting with sepsis, a patient with chest pain, and a frail elderly patient following a fall.
draft_fields.reflection:
This shift required me to balance competing priorities in a busy department. I focused on early identification of the sickest patients, the patient with sepsis received timely antibiotics and fluid resuscitation, while safely managing the diagnostic pathway for the chest pain patient and the workup for the frail faller.

My consultant observed my prioritisation, handover, and escalation decisions. Discussing these cases helped me refine my threshold for escalation and that my plans for the complex frail patient were appropriate. I learned to maintain better situational awareness when managing multiple high-acuity patients simultaneously.
key_capabilities: SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC3: will maintain situational awareness throughout the shift to ensure safety is optimised (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I acted as EPIC for a crowded evening ED shift. I allocated junior doctors, held a safety huddle, escalated capacity concerns to site management and reflected on protecting time for re-review of high-risk patients.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee acted in the EPIC (Emergency Physician in Charge) leadership role during a crowded shift, coordinating juniors, holding a safety huddle, and managing departmental capacity.
The trainee explicitly reflects on the shift, specifically regarding the cognitive challenges of protecting time for re-reviewing high-risk patients during periods of high crowding.
The trainee managed a busy department shift, oversaw the clinical workload, allocated junior doctors, and prioritised patient flow across multiple patients.

draft_fields.leadership_context: I acted as the Engineer in Charge (EPIC) during a crowded evening ED shift as an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee. The role required coordinating a busy department under significant capacity and resource pressures, managing team composition, and handling communication with wider hospital site management.
draft_fields.clinical_reasoning:
I took charge of the department, allocating junior doctors to appropriate clinical areas based on skill mix and department demand. I held a safety huddle with the multidisciplinary team to establish situational awareness, highlight high-risk patients, and delegate tasks clearly.

To manage the crowding and department flow, I escalated capacity concerns directly to hospital site management. This involved negotiating for inpatient bed flow and identifying bottlenecks within our own processes to maintain patient safety.
draft_fields.reflection:
Managing a crowded shift as EPIC requires balancing macro-level department flow with micro-level clinical oversight. That the team remained focused during peak pressure was challenging. I realised the importance of explicitly protecting time during the shift to re-review high-risk or boarding patients who are vulnerable to clinical deterioration.

I will continue to structure my EPIC shifts with regular, brief safety huddles and I build in dedicated slots to physically review the most complex patients in the department, rather than relying solely on verbal updates.
key_capabilities: SLO8 KC1: will provide support to ED staff at all levels and disciplines on the ED shift (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC3: will maintain situational awareness throughout the shift to ensure safety is optimised (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I appraised evidence on using age-adjusted D-dimer for suspected PE. I framed a PICO question, searched NICE and a recent meta-analysis, and discussed how I would apply the evidence safely in low-risk patients. I learned to document the limits of the evidence when explaining risk to patients.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee formulated a structured clinical question (PICO), searched literature (NICE, meta-analysis) to evaluate age-adjusted D-dimer in suspected PE, and appraised its safe clinical application.
The trainee includes a specific professional reflection on what they learned regarding documenting the limits of evidence when communicating risk to patients.

draft_fields.situation: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I appraised evidence on using age-adjusted D-dimer for suspected PE.
draft_fields.search_methodology: I searched NICE guidelines and a recent meta-analysis.
draft_fields.apply_to_practice: Applying the evidence safely to rule out pulmonary embolism in low-risk patients.
draft_fields.communicate_to_patient: I learned to document the limits of the evidence when explaining risk to patients.
draft_fields.reflection:
This appraisal helped me understand how to apply age-adjusted D-dimer thresholds safely in clinical practice for low-risk patients. 

I realised the importance of clear communication regarding diagnostic uncertainty. In future, I will I clearly document the limits of the evidence when discussing diagnostic risk and decision-making with patients.
key_capabilities: SLO10 KC1: be able to appraise, synthesise, communicate and use research evidence to develop EM care (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I delivered a 25-minute teaching session to four junior doctors on ECGs in chest pain. A consultant observed the session and gave feedback on pacing and checking understanding.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee delivered a formal, 25-minute structured teaching session to a group of junior doctors which was directly observed by a consultant who provided feedback on their teaching delivery.
A consultant directly observed the teaching session specifically to evaluate the trainee's teaching skills (focusing on pacing and checking learner understanding) and provided structured feedback.

key_capabilities: SLO9 KC2: be able to prepare and deliver teaching sessions outside of the clinical environment, including simulation, small group work, and didactic presentations (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I reviewed feedback from nurses, juniors and consultants. Themes were calm leadership and clear explanations, with a development point around documenting senior discussions more consistently.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee has gathered structured 360-degree feedback from multiple colleague groups (nurses, juniors, consultants) detailing leadership, communication strengths, and documentation development areas.
The trainee is actively reflecting on synthesized feedback themes (calm leadership, documentation consistency) to guide their professional development and future clinical practice.

draft_fields.context: Multi-Source Feedback (MSF) compiled from feedback provided by nursing staff, junior doctors, and consultants during my ST5 placement.
draft_fields.reflection:
I have reviewed the feedback from my MSF. It is encouraging to see positive themes regarding my calm leadership style and my ability to provide clear explanations to both patients and the multidisciplinary team.

As a development point, the feedback to document senior discussions more consistently in the patient notes. I will focus on that any advice, management plans, or escalations discussed with consultants are clearly and promptly recorded in my clinical documentation.

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I led a QI project on time to antibiotics in adult sepsis. Baseline audit was 42 cases, we introduced a triage prompt and re-audit showed improvement from 58 to 39 minutes. I presented it at governance. My PDP summary this year is to improve quality improvement leadership. Next year I will focus on embedding the prompt and supporting re-audit.

recommended_forms.rationale: QI/audit project with measurement and change; QIAT is the specific assessment form.

draft_fields.qi_engagement: I led a QI project on time to antibiotics in adult sepsis. I presented the findings and the project at the departmental governance meeting.
draft_fields.qi_understanding: This developed my practical understanding of using baseline measurement, an intervention and re-audit to assess change in an ED quality improvement project.
draft_fields.reflection:
We completed a full quality improvement cycle focusing on time to antibiotics in adult sepsis. Our baseline audit of 42 cases identified delays, so we introduced a targeted triage prompt to accelerate recognition and treatment.

The re-audit demonstrated a clear improvement, with the median time to antibiotics decreasing from 58 minutes to 39 minutes. Presenting these findings at the departmental governance meeting allowed me to share the positive impact of this system change with the wider team.
draft_fields.next_pdp: Focus on embedding the triage prompt into daily practice and supporting the next round of re-audit to sustainability.
key_capabilities: SLO11 KC1: be able to provide clinical leadership on effective Quality Improvement work (2025 Update)
SLO11 KC2: be able to support and develop a culture of departmental safety, and good clinical governance (2025 Update)
SLO12 KC2: be able to investigate a patient safety incident, participate and contribute effectively to department clinical governance activities and risk reduction processes (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I presented a journal club on CT coronary angiography in low-risk chest pain. I summarised the paper, discussed validity and explored how it might change local ED pathways.

recommended_forms.rationale: Journal club presentation or discussion described explicitly.

I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I taught two F1 doctors and a medical student a structured approach to ECG interpretation using five anonymised ECGs from the teaching file. I collected verbal feedback afterwards.

recommended_forms.rationale: The user explicitly asked for Teaching Delivered By Trainee.

draft_fields.learning_outcomes: How to apply a structured, systematic approach to ECG interpretation using clinical cases.

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I performed a shoulder reduction in ED with senior advice available but no formal DOPS. I documented neurovascular status before and after and reflected on analgesia planning.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee personally performed a shoulder reduction procedure, but no formal assessor was present to observe and sign off a DOPS.
The trainee explicitly reflected on the clinical planning of analgesia and the importance of pre- and post-procedure neurovascular documentation.

draft_fields.reflective_comments:
I performed a shoulder reduction in the emergency department with senior advice available. I that I documented the patient's neurovascular status both before and after the manipulation to monitor for any neurological or vascular compromise. 

I reflected on the analgesia planning for this procedure, considering how optimizing pain relief and muscle relaxation a smoother and less traumatic reduction. I will continue to focus on early, structured analgesia planning for future joint manipulations to improve patient comfort and procedural success.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. On ACCS I performed a lumbar puncture under supervision. I checked contraindications, used sterile technique, obtained CSF samples and reflected on patient positioning.

recommended_forms.rationale: ACCS procedural skill without a clear direct-assessment signal; use the ACCS procedural log.

draft_fields.brief_description: Under supervision, I performed a lumbar puncture. Prior to the procedure, I systematically checked for contraindications. I used a strict sterile technique to perform the puncture and successfully obtained the necessary CSF samples.
draft_fields.reflective_comments:
The procedure went well, but it highlighted the critical importance of patient positioning. Achieving optimal spinal flexion is key to opening up the interspinous spaces, which directly impacts the ease of the needle insertion.

In future procedures, I will spend more time the patient is correctly positioned and comfortable before making my first attempt, as this improves the likelihood of a successful first-pass puncture.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I completed a self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways, then applied the learning by reviewing our local traffic-light guidance and changing my safety-netting.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee independently completed a self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways to update their clinical knowledge.
The trainee is reflecting on how they applied this new learning to review local guidance and actively modify their clinical safety-netting practice.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Self-directed learning: paediatric fever pathways and safety-netting
draft_fields.resource_details: RCEMlearning module on paediatric fever pathways, alongside a review of our local paediatric traffic-light fever guidance.
draft_fields.reflection:
I completed a self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways yesterday, which prompted me to review our local traffic-light guidance for febrile children.

This learning made me reflect on how I structure my safety-netting advice for parents and carers. I realised that while I routinely assess risk using the traffic-light system, my documentation and verbal explanation of specific red flags could be more structured and explicit.

Following this review, I have changed my practice to I clearly outline precise physiological and behavioural triggers for representation (such as work of breathing, hydration status, and responsiveness) and explicitly document these safety-netting parameters in the patient record.
key_capabilities: SLO5 KC1: be expert in assessing and managing all children and young adult patients attending the ED (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I performed lung ultrasound on a breathless patient with suspected pulmonary oedema. I identified bilateral B-lines, discussed images with my consultant and reflected on integrating ultrasound with clinical assessment.

recommended_forms.rationale: Visible source documents a procedure; log the procedure unless a formal observed assessment is confirmed.
Use if an assessor directly observed the documented procedure.
The trainee personally performed and interpreted a point-of-care lung ultrasound (POCUS) to evaluate a breathless patient, making this the most specific form for the activity.

draft_fields.clinical_scenario: I assessed a breathless patient with suspected acute pulmonary oedema.
draft_fields.how_used: I performed a point-of-care lung ultrasound to assess for features of pulmonary congestion.
draft_fields.usable_images: I was able to obtain usable images, identifying bilateral B-lines.
draft_fields.interpret_images: I identified bilateral B-lines, which I then discussed with my consultant to confirm my interpretation.
draft_fields.learning_points: I reflected on the integration of ultrasound findings with my overall clinical assessment. Discussing the bilateral B-lines with my consultant helped consolidate my imaging interpretation alongside the clinical picture.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO1 KC1: to be expert in assessing and managing all adult patients attending the ED. These capabilities will apply to patients attending with both physical and psychological ill health (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I reflected on a complaint about delayed communication with a family during a busy resus shift. The clinical care was appropriate, but I should have allocated a clearer update role and documented conversations better.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee is directly reflecting on a patient/family complaint regarding communication delays, focusing on personal learning, role allocation, and documentation improvements.
As an ST5 trainee, managing and responding to complaints is a key management portfolio requirement; this allows them to document the complaint investigation from a systems and management perspective.
The entry contains strong reflective elements ('I reflected', 'I should have allocated', 'on reflection' framing) regarding non-clinical communication and operational improvements.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Reflection on complaint regarding communication during a busy resuscitation shift
draft_fields.key_features: This reflection concerns a complaint received regarding delayed communication with a family during a highly active resuscitation shift where I was working as the ST5 trainee.
draft_fields.key_aspects: The clinical care provided to the patient was appropriate. However, during a busy resus shift, I failed to allocate a clear update role to a team member and did not adequately document our communication and conversations with the family.
draft_fields.learning_points:
I learned that even when clinical management is delivered correctly, a lack of structured communication can lead to distress for families and subsequent complaints.

I need to that during high-pressure resuscitation scenarios, a specific staff member is explicitly allocated the role of updating the family, and that all updates or attempted contacts are clearly documented in the medical records.
draft_fields.further_action: I will actively assign a designated family liaison role during future resuscitation cases and a clear summary of family updates is documented in the notes. I will also discuss this case in my next educational supervision meeting to review my approach to communication during high-turnover shifts.
key_capabilities: SLO7 KC1: have expert communication skills to negotiate, manage complicated or evolving interactions (2025 Update)
SLO12 KC6: demonstrate a positive impact on the culture of the Emergency Department through attitudes and behaviours that impact positively on colleagues, patients and their relatives (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC3: manage all the life-threatening conditions including peri-arrest & arrest situations in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I contributed to a serious incident review after delayed recognition of sepsis in majors. I reviewed the timeline, identified handover gaps and learned to use an explicit trigger for senior review.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee actively contributed to a formal serious incident review regarding delayed recognition of sepsis, identifying systemic gaps and learning points.
The trainee is reflecting on a significant clinical safety event, analyzing the timeline, identifying handover gaps, and changing their future practice regarding senior review triggers.
If this review forms part of a structured management portfolio assignment investigating a clinical incident or system failure, the management project form is appropriate.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Serious Incident Review: Delayed Recognition of Sepsis
draft_fields.description: I contributed to a serious incident review following the delayed recognition of sepsis in a patient who was located in the majors area of the Emergency Department.
draft_fields.root_causes: After reviewing the timeline of the patient's journey, we identified critical handover gaps as a root cause of the delay in recognising and treating the patient's sepsis.
draft_fields.contributing_factors: Contributing factors included communication failures during staff handovers and the lack of a structured, explicit trigger protocol for escalating deteriorating patients to a senior clinician.
draft_fields.learning_points: I learned the critical importance of , structured handover processes to prevent clinical deterioration from being missed. I also learned that utilizing explicit, objective triggers is essential to prompt immediate senior review in suspected sepsis.
draft_fields.further_action: I will change my practice by all handovers I participate in are structured and clear. I will actively implement and advocate for explicit senior review triggers for any patient in majors showing signs of early deterioration or suspected sepsis.
key_capabilities: SLO11 KC2: be able to support and develop a culture of departmental safety, and good clinical governance (2025 Update)
SLO12 KC2: be able to investigate a patient safety incident, participate and contribute effectively to department clinical governance activities and risk reduction processes (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I attended a regional paediatric emergency medicine teaching day covering safeguarding, sepsis and non-accidental injury. I reflected on using the learning in future paediatric assessments.

recommended_forms.rationale: The user explicitly asked for Teaching Delivered By Trainee.

draft_fields.learning_points:
I attended a regional paediatric emergency medicine teaching day covering safeguarding, sepsis, and non-accidental injury.

I reflected on how to integrate this updated knowledge into my future paediatric assessments, screening for safeguarding and non-accidental injuries while maintaining a high index of suspicion for early paediatric sepsis.

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I completed ATLS today. The course refreshed my trauma team approach, primary survey and communication with the team leader. I will apply the structured handover in resus.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee completed a structured, certified ATLS course today which is a formal requirement and fits this form type perfectly.
The trainee is reflecting on their personal learning from the course, specifically how it refreshed their trauma team approach and how they plan to apply the structured handover in practice.

draft_fields.project_description: I completed the Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) course today.
draft_fields.reflective_notes: The course refreshed my trauma team approach, the systematic primary survey, and effective communication with the team leader. I reflected on how a structured approach is vital during high-pressure trauma resuscitations to no life-threatening injuries are missed.
draft_fields.lessons_learned: I will apply the structured handover in resus and maintain a systematic primary survey approach during trauma presentations.

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. My consultant assessed me during a three-hour evening ED shift. I managed flow in majors, supported juniors, reviewed two unwell patients and reflected on maintaining situational awareness under pressure.

recommended_forms.rationale: A consultant observed and assessed your non-technical skills, departmental flow management, and junior support across a three-hour shift period.
Allows you to record your personal reflection on maintaining situational awareness and managing cognitive load under pressure during a busy shift.

draft_fields.reflection:
During this three-hour evening shift, I acted as the shift leader to manage patient flow in majors while providing clinical oversight. I supported junior colleagues with their management plans and directly reviewed two unwell patients who required urgent escalation and stabilisation. 

This shift highlighted the difficulty of balancing direct clinical care of complex patients with the broader oversight required to maintain departmental safety. When reviewing unwell patients, it is easy to lose situational awareness of the wider department. 

I focused on maintaining a visible presence, regularly updating the nurse in charge, and checking in with juniors. In future shifts, I will continue to practice structuring my reviews of unwell patients so I can quickly hand over or delegate tasks, I can step back and maintain an overview of departmental flow and safety.
key_capabilities: SLO8 KC1: will provide support to ED staff at all levels and disciplines on the ED shift (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC3: will maintain situational awareness throughout the shift to ensure safety is optimised (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I managed a breathless patient where I initially anchored on COPD. Senior review prompted a PE workup. I learned to pause when the story does not fit and to document my differential more explicitly.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a patient with diagnostic complexity, where they initially anchored on COPD before a senior-prompted workup revealed a pulmonary embolism. This retrospective discussion of clinical reasoning, differential diagnosis, and anchoring bias is ideal for a Case-Based Discussion.

The trainee explicitly reflects on cognitive bias ('anchored on COPD'), discusses learning to pause when clinical features do not align, and plans to change future practice regarding differential diagnosis documentation.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Reflecting on anchoring bias in a breathless patient
draft_fields.reflection:
I managed a breathless patient where I initially anchored on COPD. I focused on the patient's history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and began standard treatment.

During a senior review with the consultant, they challenged my working diagnosis because certain elements of the presentation did not fit. This prompted a workup for a pulmonary embolism.
draft_fields.replay_differently: I would explicitly document a broader differential diagnosis and build in a deliberate pause to review the clinical features that do not fit my primary hypothesis.
draft_fields.why: I anchored too quickly on a common pre-existing condition, which narrowed my initial diagnostic focus and created a cognitive blind spot.
draft_fields.different_outcome: The clinical outcome was safe due to the timely senior review, but a more structured diagnostic approach from the start would have led to an earlier pulmonary embolism workup and reduced diagnostic delay.
draft_fields.focussing_on: I am focussing on using a deliberate re-evaluation pause to challenge my working diagnosis when reviewing patients with acute breathlessness who have chronic respiratory histories.
draft_fields.learned: I have learned how easily anchoring bias can occur in patients with chronic respiratory conditions, and the importance of actively looking for features that contradict my initial working diagnosis.
key_capabilities: SLO1 KC1: to be expert in assessing and managing all adult patients attending the ED. These capabilities will apply to patients attending with both physical and psychological ill health (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. A consultant observed me teaching an F2 doctor how to assess ankle injuries. They fed back that my explanations were clear but I should check the learner's baseline knowledge first.

recommended_forms.rationale: The user explicitly asked for Teaching Delivered By Trainee.

draft_fields.reflection:
An Emergency Medicine consultant observed me teaching an F2 doctor how to assess ankle injuries. The consultant fed back that my explanations were clear, but advised that I should check the learner's baseline knowledge first before delivering the content.

In future teaching sessions, I will actively ask the learner what they already know about the topic at the start. This will allow me to tailor my teaching more effectively to their specific learning needs rather than assuming their level of understanding.

recommended_forms.rationale: The user explicitly asked for Audit Assessment Tool.

I completed an audit of the documentation of capacity assessments in ED mental health presentations. I reviewed 30 case notes to evaluate current practice against established guidelines.

This review demonstrated clear gaps in how capacity assessments are recorded. By presenting these results to the department and proposing a dedicated documentation prompt, I aim to improve compliance and patient safety. I will monitor the uptake of this prompt to see if documentation quality improves.

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I recruited patients to an ED research study after completing GCP training. I checked eligibility, explained the study, obtained consent and reflected on protecting clinical flow.

recommended_forms.rationale: Perfect fit for ST5 management portfolio requirements, specifically demonstrating active involvement in clinical trial recruitment, patient screening, and consent processes.
Captures the trainee's reflection on maintaining clinical flow, balancing research recruitment with active service delivery, and applying GCP training to real-world practice.

draft_fields.reflection: I recruited patients to an ED research study yesterday. During the shift, I identified suitable patients, checked their eligibility against the study criteria, explained the trial process clearly, and obtained formal informed consent. This required balancing active research recruitment with maintaining clinical flow in a busy emergency department. I had to that dedicating time to the consent process did not compromise patient care or departmental throughput. I will continue to look for opportunities to integrate clinical research into my daily practice efficiently.
key_capabilities: SLO10 KC2: be able to actively participate in research (2025 Update)
